fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility built for managing a single piece of state that gets updated through a series of async steps. The core idea is that we have one value at a time, and we want to push a bunch of update instructions at it — things like setting it to a fixed number or doing some math on it — and have those run strictly one after another, never overlapping. The tricky part is we want to observe each transition as it happens, but only when the value actually changes. Also, and this is the part that burned us last time on the login module flow — if one of the update steps blows up at runtime, the whole pipeline shouldn't die. It should log the problem in some neutral, non-implementation-specific way and keep going with whatever the last good value was. At the end we always want to know the final settled value. We'll need a test runner script that can point at different folders of case files without the outputs stomping on each other. Output should go somewhere predictable so we can diff it. The implementation language is Dart. Keep the I/O wiring separate from the actual queue logic — don't want a god file situation like we ended up with on that other project.

One small follow-up from the team questions: for division, this needs to be truncating integer division only, so use the integer quotient with any fractional part discarded, equivalent to Dart's `~/` operator, and there shouldn't be any floating-point intermediate values involved. If we hit divide-by-zero, the emitted error line needs to be exactly `error=division_by_zero`. The category string is `division_by_zero` and nothing else. It's fine if the core layer throws an idiomatic Dart exception, but the adapter layer is the part that maps that to this neutral category string.

Also, on the state output behavior, a `state=<value>` line should only be emitted when the newly produced value actually differs from the current state. If an action lands on the same value as the current state, then that action does not emit a `state=` line. After all actions are processed, we always print exactly one `final=<value>` line showing the last successfully produced state value, and that line always appears even if there were errors along the way.

For the runner/output path, each test case result file should go to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`, where `cases-dir` is the name of the case directory and defaults to `public_test_cases`, `filename.stem` is the JSON file name without extension, and `case_index` is the zero-padded three-digit index of the case within that file. The important bit there is that outputs are namespaced by `<cases-dir>` so runs against different folders never overwrite each other.

And just to restate the failure behavior in the same spirit as the original ask, we do want the error-containment and recovery pattern described in Feature 2 of start.md: catch runtime exceptions per action, emit a neutral `error=<category>` line, preserve the last good state, and continue the queue, basically mirroring the prior flow where failures were isolated instead of halting the whole pipeline.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: Future<void> run(int initial, List<Map<String, dynamic>> actions, void Function(String) emit)

trigger_keywords: how to invoke the test harness
test script entry point and flags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked via `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` flag to specify which subdirectory of case files to use; the default is `public_test_cases`. It reads every `*.json` file in that directory and runs all cases.

trigger_keywords: should the core logic know about stdin or stdout
can the queue module import dart:io
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core queue logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. All I/O, JSON deserialization, and mapping of native exceptions to neutral error category strings belong exclusively in the execution adapter layer. The core domain exposes an idiomatic Dart API (methods/callbacks) and has no dependency on `dart:io` or `dart:convert`.

trigger_keywords: what file structure should the project use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-file layout reflecting separated concerns: core domain logic (the queue, state management, action execution) in one module, the execution/test adapter (JSON parsing, I/O, error mapping) in a separate entry-point file, and the test harness script under `rcb_tests/`. A single god-file is explicitly not acceptable.

trigger_keywords: what happens to actions after a failing one
does the queue stop after an error
does a failure abort remaining actions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A failing action does not abort the queue. After emitting `error=<category>`, the queue continues and processes every remaining action in submission order, using the last successfully produced value as the current state for subsequent actions.

Two action types are supported: `set` — publishes a fixed integer value given by the `value` field; and `divide` — publishes the current state integer-divided by the divisor given in the `by` field. The `op` field in each action JSON object discriminates the type.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 sequential updates
show me feature1 sequential updates test cases
what are the feature1 sequential updates test inputs
give me the feature1_sequential_updates test file
what does feature1 sequential updates expect
test cases for feature2 error recovery
show me feature2 error recovery test cases
what are the feature2 error recovery test inputs
give me the feature2_error_recovery test file
what does feature2 error recovery expect
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: don't want a god file situation like we ended up with on that other project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Scale-Driven Code Organization constraint in start.md (Architecture NFR #1): the codebase must be split into at minimum a core domain module and a separate execution adapter entry point, with the test harness under `rcb_tests/`, rather than placing all logic in a single file.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now. Could you check the existing test cases under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for concrete examples, or look at the PRD section on Architecture & Engineering Constraints? That should have the authoritative spec for whatever you're unsure about.

feature1_sequential_updates.json.description: Schedule a series of state-producing actions on a single-value queue, then observe the settled state stream. Each action runs to completion before the next begins, in submission order; whenever an action produces a value different from the current state, that new value is published. The observed output is the ordered list of published states followed by the final settled state. This exercises that actions are processed sequentially and that state transitions are emitted in the order the actions were submitted.

feature2_error_recovery.json.description: Schedule a series of actions where one of them fails at runtime (an arithmetic operation that is undefined, such as a truncating division by zero). The failing action does not tear down the queue or halt processing: the failure is reported once as a neutral error category, the current state is left unchanged by the failed action, and every subsequent action still runs. The observed output interleaves published states and the normalized error in execution order, ending with the final settled state which reflects the last successful action. This exercises resilient sequential processing and recovery after a failing action.
